exemplar.title: How does the different material composition in feather and plastic shuttlecocks affect their velocities?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the different material composition in feather and plastic shuttlecocks affect their velocities?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, sharply focused on velocity, and consistently drives the theoretical and experimental sections.
Excellent use of headings, table of contents, and signposting to maintain a coherent line of inquiry.
Methodology is comprehensive: equipment choice, calibration, uncertainty analysis, and literature values are all justified and integrated effectively.
weaknesses: Occasional drift into peripheral topics (range, preference) in the introduction, which could be more tightly tied back to velocity throughout.
The description of sampling as âsystematic randomâ overstates randomness and could be more critically discussed vis-Ã -vis bias.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place topic clarity firmly in the top band (5â6), research question in the upper band (5), and methodology at band 5. Taken together, the clarity, focus, and methodological completeness warrant the maximum score of 6 in Focus and Method. Minor lapses (e.g., slight wording imprecision, over-stated sampling randomness) remain peripheral to the investigationâs overall rigor and focus.

strengths: Solid demonstration of key physics concepts (drag coefficient, Reynolds number) and application of formulas to data.
Terminology (terminal velocity, drag force) is generally accurate, symbols defined, and units attached.
Frequent linkage of theoretical models to experimental expectations.
weaknesses: Several numerical results are dimensionally inconsistent or physically unrealistic (e.g., net forces on a 5 g mass).
Inconsistencies in symbol usage (U vs. v, S vs. A) and occasional speed/velocity interchange.
Explanations, while clear, sometimes lack depth in relating theory to material differences beyond velocity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and Understanding strands were both assessed at band 3â4 (upper value 4). The student shows good subject knowledge and mostly accurate terminology, but dimensional errors and inconsistent notation prevent elevation to band 5â6. A score of 4 reflects this strong yet imperfect performance.

strengths: The analysis consistently focuses on the research question, integrating primary data with theoretical drag and Reynolds-number models.
Arguments progress logically from motivation through theory, data analysis, and interpretation, with clear narrative coherence.
Critical evaluation identifies key methodological limitations and suggests plausible, focused improvements.
weaknesses: Arithmetic inaccuracies and small sample size slightly undermine the rigor of the analysis.
Some leaps in reasoning (e.g., relating net force directly to flight speed without reconciling drag calculations) remain unbridged.
Evaluation, though insightful, could probe deeper statistical significance and error-propagation effects.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed analysis and evaluation in band 4â6 (scores 5 and 4) and argument development in the same band (5). While not flawless, the studentâs research analysis and argumentation are well above basic levels and close to the excellent criteria. Awarding 11/12 acknowledges their sophisticated integration of theory and data, coherent structure, and substantive critical reflection, deducting a single mark for the noted analytical and evaluative gaps.

strengths: Clear, well-formatted structure with all EE conventions met (title page, word count, numbering, bibliography).
Figures and tables are appropriately placed with captions, aiding reader comprehension.
Layout and formatting support readability and navigation throughout the report.
weaknesses: A few images (survey chart, uncertainty formula) are low resolution or overly dense, which can impede clarity.
Minor typographical inconsistencies in minus signs and equation formatting.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation strands were assessed at band 3â4, with structure in band 3â4 (score 3) and layout in band 3â4 (score 4). The overall adherence to formatting conventions and effective visual communication justify the top score of 4 in Presentation, with only small formatting/clarity issues preventing a perfect visual delivery.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Physics vocabulary such as âterminal velocity,â âdrag coefficient,â and âaerodynamic lengthâ is used frequently and generally correctly.  Symbols are defined and units are usually attached.  

Nonetheless, the essay sometimes interchanges speed/velocity, mislabels vector arrows, and introduces typographical inconsistencies (U vs v), showing that terminology is mostly but not fully accurate.  These features align with the 3-4 descriptor; a score of 4 is awarded because errors are occasional rather than pervasive.

The argument proceeds logically from survey motivation â theoretical background â experimental results â interpretation.  Tables and equations are woven into the narrative, and the conclusion references earlier predictions, evidencing a coherent structure.

However, some reasoning leaps remain (e.g. equating greater net force with flight speed without reconciling drag calculations) and numerical mistakes diminish overall coherence.  Consequently the discussion is well-developed but not sophisticated, justifying a band 4-6 score of 5.

The methodology is fully described: equipment choice (mechanical feeder, iPhone camera, Tracker software), controlled variables, calibration steps and uncertainty treatment are all presented.  The inclusion of theoretical models, literature values for C_D, and explicit equations shows informed selection of both primary and secondary sources.

Limitations (e.g. 30 fps camera, launcher accuracy) are acknowledged, indicating awareness of methodological adequacy.  Nevertheless, only five trials per shuttlecock and reliance on low-frame-rate video leave some gaps in completeness, keeping the strand at 5 rather than 6.

The student demonstrates solid understanding of projectile motion, drag and Reynolds number and applies them to shuttlecock flight.  Equations are derived and linked to data, and literature coefficients are incorporated appropriately.  

However, several numerical results are unrealistic (e.g. net forces of ~50 N on a 5 g mass) and dimensional inconsistencies occasionally appear.  These errors indicate lapses in coherent understanding and limit the work to the 3-4 band; the depth of explanation merits the upper value of 4 within that band.

The student identifies methodological limitations (launcher accuracy, frame rate, lack of wind tunnel) and suggests plausible improvements.  The mismatch between survey preference and measured performance is explored, showing some critical perspective.

Nevertheless, evaluation is brief relative to the length of the report and does not probe deeper issues such as calculation errors or statistical significance.  The critique is therefore âgoodâ but not sustained enough for higher bands, warranting a low-band 4-6 score of 4.

The student frames the investigation around the clearly stated research question: âHow does the different material composition in feather and plastic shuttlecocks affect their velocities?â  The title, abstract-style opening, and early survey results all orient the reader unambiguously toward this purpose.  Throughout the report the student signposts the link between each section (theory, design, analysis) and the stated goal, maintaining a consistent focus.

Key physics terms (drag coefficient, Reynolds number, aerodynamic length) are introduced early with concise definitions, and the reader is told exactly why each idea matters to the question of velocity.  Headings and the table of contents reinforce the single, coherent line of inquiry.  Because the communication is both accurate and purposeful, the work fits solidly in the 5-6 band for topic clarity.

Figures are embedded near relevant text and captions are provided; Tracker screenshots and photographs help the reader visualise procedures.  However, several images are low resolution and axes on graphs are sometimes hard to read, slightly limiting effectiveness.  Despite these minor flaws, the layout generally supports communication, fitting comfortably into the 3-4 band; a score of 3 is assigned to reflect the small inconsistencies.

The research question is explicitly phrased, focused on a single measurable variable (velocity) and directly shapes the experimental design and theoretical background.  All subsequent calculations, data tables and discussion return to this one dependent variable, showing a strong connection between question and discussion.

Minor breadth is introduced when the student briefly references other properties (range, preference), but these are used only to justify the choice of velocity, not to dilute the focus. Hence the work meets the descriptors for a clear, well-focused RQ, meriting the upper band; a score of 5 reflects slight lapses in wording precision (e.g. occasionally using âspeedâ and âvelocityâ interchangeably).

Primary data are collected, statistically processed, and compared with theoretical expectations; the student then extends the analysis to drag force and Reynolds number, showing clear relevance to the RQ.  Graphs, fitted functions and integrations illustrate reasonable analytical effort.

Yet the small sample size, modest frame rate and some arithmetic inaccuracies weaken the rigour.  The analysis is therefore âgoodâ rather than âexcellent,â fitting the 4-6 band; midpoint 5 reflects balanced strengths and weaknesses.

The essay follows expected EE conventions: title page with word count, table of contents, sectional headings, numbered tables and figures, bibliography and appendices.  Pagination appears consistent, and equations are formatted clearly.  Overall the structure supports readability and meets all formal requirements, justifying the top band (3-4) with a score of 4.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the investigationâs focus but uses inconsistent capitalization and âVsâ instead of the conventional âvs.â Standardize title case and use âvs.â for clarity.
Word count is provided and falls within the 4000-word limit, demonstrating compliance with formatting requirements.
The research question is concise and focused on a single measurable variable (velocity), establishing clear direction for theoretical and experimental sections.
Expand on how the drag coefficient varies with Reynolds number and link that to the shuttlecockâs material to deepen theoretical context.
Heading levels jump between h2 and h3 in this section; ensure uniform heading hierarchy to maintain a coherent document structure.
Presenting the drag force formula $$F_D = \tfrac12\rho C_DS U^2$$ provides a solid theoretical foundation for your velocity analysis.
The reference-area measurement image lacks orientation detail; specify the axis used and calibration in the figure caption for reproducibility.
Area is denoted as \(S\) earlier but equation (4) uses \(A\). Unify symbol usage to avoid confusion between reference area and cross-sectional area.
The final dynamics model $$F = m\vec g - \tfrac12\rho SC_DU^2$$ neatly integrates gravity and drag, effectively linking theory to your experimental question.
When comparing shuttlecock models, include reference to manufacturer specifications (mass distribution, skirt porosity) to justify assumptions.
Ignoring uncertainty for literature-taken area may underrepresent error propagation; discuss potential impact of source variance on results.
The multi-term uncertainty formula is dense and could overwhelm readers; break it into incremental steps with annotations for clarity.
Connecting terminal velocity theory to the shuttlecockâs steady-state fall strengthens anticipation of experimental trends.
Table of contents is comprehensive and well-formatted, aiding navigation and reflecting careful structuring of the essayâs sections.
The introduction provides cultural context but drifts from the research question. Refocus to emphasize physics relevance earlier to strengthen coherence.
The claim that humidity-induced brittleness affects shuttlecock flight lacks supporting evidence. Provide a source or test to link material condition to velocity.
Labeling the sampling as âsystematic randomâ may overstate its randomness when respondents come from a single tournament; discuss how this impacts bias.
The survey bar chart is low resolution and axis labels are difficult to read; replace with higher-quality graphics for clear data interpretation.
Interchanging âvelocityâ and âspeedâ may confuse vector versus scalar concepts; explicitly define velocity and speed, and use terms consistently.
You note that air resistance and rotational motion will be considered, but expand on how rotational dynamics specifically influence shuttlecock flight.
Vector velocity is denoted as \(\vec U\) here but later uses \(v\). Standardize your symbol for velocity throughout the essay.
Thresholds for turbulent and laminar flow (Re > 4000, Re < 2000) should be formatted in equation form (e.g. $$Re>4000$$) to improve readability.
The image measuring shuttlecock length lacks a scale or reference; include a ruler or grid overlay to validate the reported aerodynamic length.
Uncertainty of \(\pm0.1\times10^{-3}\) m is unclear; explain how the lowest increment of the device translates to this uncertainty value.
Negative values in the processed data table use hyphens not minus signs; ensure proper mathematical minus symbols for scientific accuracy.
